attributes.stem:
An otherwise well 81-year-old man is referred to a specialist clinic with a four-year history of intermittent difficulty in swallowing. His symptoms have become more noticeable over the last 12 months. He has no trouble starting to swallow his food, but it is now associated with a cough and episodes of choking. He coughs up pieces of undigested food and tablets that he has recently taken. He has lost two kilograms in the last 12 months. He reports no chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, or nausea.

On examination his BMI is 20.1, with a blood pressure of 144/86 mmHg, pulse 80/min and temperature 37.4C. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate initial investigation?
attributes.explanation:
Dysphagia can generally be divided into two types; oropharyngeal and oesophageal. Oropharyngeal dysphagia is difficulty initiating swallow; whereas oesophageal dysphagia is difficulty in passing the material down the oesophagus. From the history alone, it is clear that this patient has oesophageal dysphagia. His weight loss should always raise the possibility of malignancy, but the length of the history would make this a quite unlikely diagnosis. Coughing up particles of food and other small materials such as tablets is very suggestive that the material is getting caught in the upper oesophagus and being inadvertently inhaled. This clinical picture would fit very well for a pharyngeal pouch (Zenker's diverticulum). Whilst these diverticula can be visualised with endoscopy, a much more accurate assessment is made with a contrast study (barium swallow). In terms of the practicalities of arranging diagnostic investigations, it will usually be much easier to get the imaging study done first.

If the presumed diagnosis was one of oesophageal malignancy, then an endoscopy would be the most appropriate first-line investigation. Apart from viewing the lumen of the upper digestive tract, any suspicious lesion could be biopsied. However, in this case the length of the history would make malignancy a very unusual diagnosis.

24-hour pH and manometry is an appropriate investigation when the diagnosis s thought to be gastro-oesophageal reflux disease or a motility disorder. Neither of these two problems is likely to be the cause of this patient's dysphagia. Manometry would not be a first-line investigation, but could follow a contrast study or endoscopy.

CT chest is not considered a first-line investigation in dysphagia, although it is performed with increasing frequency in cases of suspected oesophageal malignancy - often for expediency's sake, where there can be delays in getting an endoscopy done.

A bronchoscopy may be useful in the diagnosis of an apical lung tumour, which can cause dysphagia, but is far less common. In such cases, a CT of the chest would precede the bronchoscopy.
learningPoints: A contrast study should be the first-line investigation in cases of suspected oesophageal diverticula or motility disorders.
references: Law R, Katzka DA, Baron TH. Zenker's Diverticulum. Clinical Gastroenterol Hepatol 2014;12:1773–82.
